content:
# Joshua Chapter 5 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 228-229 | **Source Images:** `../../source-texts/hebrew-OT/JOS_005_page_001.jpg`, `JOS_005_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 165-166 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0169

---

*The Kings Hear and Their Hearts Melt (v. 1)*

**5:1** And it was [וַיְהִי] when all the kings of the Amorites [כְּשְׁמֹעַ כָּל-מַלְכֵי הָאֱמֹרִי] who [were] across the Jordan [אֲשֶׁר בְּעֵבֶר הַיַּרְדֵּן] to the sea [יָמָּה] and all the kings of the Canaanites [וְכָל-מַלְכֵי הַכְּנַעֲנִי] who [were] by the sea [אֲשֶׁר עַל-הַיָּם] heard [שָׁמְעוּ] that YHWH had dried up [אֲשֶׁר-הוֹבִישׁ יְהוָה] the waters of the Jordan [אֶת-מֵי הַיַּרְדֵּן] from before the sons of Israel [מִפְּנֵי בְנֵי-יִשְׂרָאֵל] until they crossed over [עַד-עָבְרָם] — and their heart melted [וַיִּמַּס לְבָבָם] and there was no longer [וְלֹא-הָיָה] spirit [רוּחַ] in them [בָם עוֹד] from before [מִפְּנֵי] the sons of Israel [בְּנֵי-יִשְׂרָאֵל].

---

*YHWH Commands the Second Circumcision (vv. 2–3)*

**5:2** At that time [בָּעֵת הַהִיא] YHWH said [אָמַר יְהוָה] to Joshua [אֶל-יְהוֹשֻׁעַ]: Make [עֲשֵׂה] for yourself [לְךָ] knives of flint [חַרְבוֹת צֻרִים] and return [וְשׁוּב], circumcise [מֹל] the sons of Israel [אֶת-בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל] a second time [שֵׁנִית].

**5:3** And Joshua made [וַיַּעַשׂ-לוֹ יְהוֹשֻׁעַ] for himself knives of flint [חַרְבוֹת צֻרִים] and he circumcised [וַיָּמָל] the sons of Israel [אֶת-בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל] at the Hill of the Foreskins [אֶל גִּבְעַת הָעֲרָלוֹת].

---

*The Reason for the Second Circumcision (vv. 4–7)*

**5:4** And this [is] the reason [וְזֶה הַדָּבָר] that Joshua circumcised [אֲשֶׁר-מָל יְהוֹשֻׁעַ]: all the people [כָּל-הָעָם] who went out [הַיֹּצֵא] from Egypt [מִמִּצְרַיִם] — the males [הַזְּכָרִים] — all the men of war [כֹּל אַנְשֵׁי הַמִּלְחָמָה] — died [מֵתוּ] in the wilderness [בַּמִּדְבָּר] on the way [בַּדֶּרֶךְ] in their going out [בְּצֵאתָם] from Egypt [מִמִּצְרָיִם].

**5:5** For circumcised [כִּי-מֻלִים] were all the people [הָיוּ כָּל-הָעָם] who went out [הַיֹּצְאִים] and all the people [וְכָל-הָעָם] who were born [הַיִּלֹּדִים] in the wilderness [בַּמִּדְבָּר] on the way [בַּדֶּרֶךְ] in their going out [בְּצֵאתָם] from Egypt [מִמִּצְרַיִם] — they had not circumcised [לֹא-מָלוּ].

**5:6** For forty years [כִּי אַרְבָּעִים שָׁנָה] the sons of Israel walked [הָלְכוּ בְנֵי-יִשְׂרָאֵל] in the wilderness [בַּמִּדְבָּר] until all the nation [עַד-תֹּם כָּל-הַגּוֹי] — the men of war [אַנְשֵׁי הַמִּלְחָמָה] — who went out from Egypt [הַיֹּצְאִים מִמִּצְרַיִם] — were finished [תַּמּוּ] — who did not listen [אֲשֶׁר לֹא-שָׁמְעוּ] to the voice of YHWH [בְּקוֹל יְהוָה] — to whom YHWH swore [אֲשֶׁר נִשְׁבַּע יְהוָה] not to show them [לְבִלְתִּי הַרְאוֹתָם] the land [אֶת-הָאָרֶץ] that YHWH swore [אֲשֶׁר נִשְׁבַּע יְהוָה] to their fathers [לַאֲבוֹתָם] to give to us [לָתֶת-לָנוּ] — a land flowing [אֶרֶץ זָבַת] milk and honey [חָלָב וּדְבָשׁ].

**5:7** And their sons [וְאֶת-בְּנֵיהֶם] he raised up [הֵקִים] in their place [תַּחְתָּם] — them Joshua circumcised [אֹתָם מָל יְהוֹשֻׁעַ] — for uncircumcised they were [כִּי-עֲרֵלִים הָיוּ] — for they had not circumcised them [כִּי לֹא-מָלוּ אוֹתָם] on the way [בַּדָּרֶךְ].

---

*The Reproach of Egypt Rolled Away (vv. 8–9)*

**5:8** And it was [וַיְהִי] when all the nation [כַּאֲשֶׁר-תַּמּוּ כָל-הַגּוֹי] had finished being circumcised [לְהִמּוֹל] and they remained [וַיֵּשְׁבוּ] in their place [תַּחְתָּם] in the camp [בַּמַּחֲנֶה] until they recovered [עַד חֲיוֹתָם].

**5:9** And YHWH said [וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה] to Joshua [אֶל-יְהוֹשֻׁעַ]: Today [הַיּוֹם] I have rolled away [גַּלּוֹתִי] the reproach of Egypt [אֶת-חֶרְפַּת מִצְרַיִם] from upon you [מֵעֲלֵיכֶם]. And he called [וַיִּקְרָא] the name of that place [שֵׁם הַמָּקוֹם הַהוּא] Gilgal [גִּלְגָּל] until this day [עַד הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה].

---

*The Passover at Gilgal and the Manna Ceases (vv. 10–12)*

**5:10** And the sons of Israel encamped [וַיַּחֲנוּ בְנֵי-יִשְׂרָאֵל] in Gilgal [בַּגִּלְגָּל] and they made [וַיַּעֲשׂוּ] the Passover [אֶת-הַפֶּסַח] on the fourteenth day [בְּאַרְבָּעָה עָשָׂר יוֹם] of the month [לַחֹדֶשׁ] in the evening [בָּעֶרֶב] in the plains of Jericho [בְּעַרְבוֹת יְרִיחוֹ].

**5:11** And they ate [וַיֹּאכְלוּ] from the produce of the land [מֵעֲבוּר הָאָרֶץ] on the morrow of the Passover [מִמָּחֳרַת הַפֶּסַח] — unleavened cakes [מַצּוֹת] and parched grain [וְקָלוּי] — in the bone of this very day [בְּעֶצֶם הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה].

**5:12** And the manna ceased [וַיִּשְׁבֹּת הַמָּן] on the morrow [מִמָּחֳרָת] when they ate [בְּאָכְלָם] from the produce of the land [מֵעֲבוּר הָאָרֶץ] and there was no more [וְלֹא-הָיָה עוֹד] manna [מָן] for the sons of Israel [לִבְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל] and they ate [וַיֹּאכְלוּ] from the yield of the land of Canaan [מִתְּבוּאַת אֶרֶץ כְּנַעַן] in that year [בַּשָּׁנָה הַהִיא].

---

*The Commander of the Army of YHWH (vv. 13–15)*

**5:13** And it was [וַיְהִי] when Joshua was [בִּהְיוֹת יְהוֹשֻׁעַ] by Jericho [בִּירִיחוֹ] and he lifted up his eyes [וַיִּשָּׂא עֵינָיו] and he saw [וַיַּרְא] and look [וְהִנֵּה] — a man [אִישׁ] standing [עֹמֵד] before him [לְנֶגְדּוֹ] and his sword [וְחַרְבּוֹ] drawn [שְׁלוּפָה] in his hand [בְּיָדוֹ]. And Joshua went [וַיֵּלֶךְ יְהוֹשֻׁעַ] to him [אֵלָיו] and said to him [וַיֹּאמֶר לוֹ]: Are you for us [הֲלָנוּ אַתָּה] or for our adversaries [אִם-לְצָרֵינוּ]?

**5:14** And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: No [לֹא] — for I [am] [כִּי אֲנִי] commander of the army of YHWH [שַׂר-צְבָא-יְהוָה] — now I have come [עַתָּה בָאתִי]. And Joshua fell [וַיִּפֹּל יְהוֹשֻׁעַ] on his face [אֶל-פָּנָיו] to the ground [אָרְצָה] and bowed down [וַיִּשְׁתָּחוּ] and said to him [וַיֹּאמֶר לוֹ]: What [does] my lord [מָה אֲדֹנִי] speak [מְדַבֵּר] to his servant [אֶל-עַבְדּוֹ]?

**5:15** And the commander of the army of YHWH said [וַיֹּאמֶר שַׂר-צְבָא-יְהוָה] to Joshua [אֶל-יְהוֹשֻׁעַ]: Remove [שַׁל] your sandal [נַעַלְךָ] from upon your foot [מֵעַל רַגְלֶךָ] — for the place [כִּי הַמָּקוֹם] upon which you [are] standing [אֲשֶׁר אַתָּה עֹמֵד עָלָיו] — holy it [is] [קֹדֶשׁ הוּא]. And Joshua did so [וַיַּעַשׂ יְהוֹשֻׁעַ כֵּן].

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

### On 5:1 — וַיִּמַּס לְבָבָם: Their Heart Melted

**וַיִּמַּס לְבָבָם** — "and their heart melted."

The verb **מָסַס** (masas, "to melt, dissolve, become liquid") is the same verb used by Rahab in 2:11: "and our hearts melted [וַיִּמַּס לְבָבֵנוּ] and there was no longer spirit [רוּחַ] in any man from before you." The narrative fulfills Rahab's testimony: what she reported as the state of Jericho's inhabitants now extends to all the kings of the Amorites and the Canaanites. The drying up of the Jordan (chapter 4) completes the parallel with the drying up of the Sea of Reeds — both events are water-crossings on dry ground, and both produce the melting of enemy hearts. The phrase **וְלֹא-הָיָה בָם עוֹד רוּחַ** — "and there was no longer spirit in them" — indicates a collapse of will, a loss of the animating breath of courage. **רוּחַ** (ruach) here carries the sense of inner resolve, fighting spirit.

The Qere/Ketiv situation in 5:1 is notable: the Ketiv has **עָבְרֵנוּ** ("until we crossed over" — first person plural), while the Qere reads **עָבְרָם** ("until they crossed over" — third person). The Ketiv preserves a striking perspective shift: the narrator momentarily identifies with the crossing generation ("until we crossed"), placing himself among the Israelites who experienced the Jordan crossing. The EMET follows the Qere (**עָבְרָם**) as standard but notes the Ketiv as a remarkable textual witness to the living voice of the tradition.

---

### On 5:2 — חַרְבוֹת צֻרִים: Knives of Flint (Key Crux)

**חַרְבוֹת צֻרִים** — "knives of flint."

The word **חַרְבוֹת** (charevot) is the plural of **חֶרֶב** (cherev, "sword, knife, cutting instrument"). The word **צֻרִים** (tsurim) means "flints" — from **צוּר** (tsur, "rock, flint"). Flint knives are specified rather than bronze or iron instruments. This is not merely an archaism — flint was used for circumcision specifically in the ancient Near East, and the specification of flint in this context connects to Exodus 4:25 where Zipporah uses a **צֹר** (tsor, "flint") to circumcise Moses's son. The use of stone implements for sacred rituals is well attested in the ancient Near East — the ritual conservatism of circumcision preserved the use of flint long after metal tools were available for ordinary use.

**שֵׁנִית** — "a second time." This does not mean each individual was circumcised twice, but that the nation as a whole underwent circumcision for the second time — the first generation having been circumcised in Egypt, the second generation (born in the wilderness) now receiving the rite.

The LXX (Codex Vaticanus) renders **μαχαίρας πετρίνας** — "stone knives" — using **πέτρινος** (petrinos, "made of stone/rock") for **צֻרִים**. The Greek also adds **ἐκ πέτρας ἀκροτόμου** ("from sharp stone") in some recensions, expanding the Hebrew.

---

### On 5:3 — גִּבְעַת הָעֲרָלוֹת: The Hill of the Foreskins

**גִּבְעַת הָעֲרָלוֹת** — "the Hill of the Foreskins."

This place-name is a topographical etiology: the hill near Gilgal received its name from the mass circumcision that occurred there. The name is startlingly literal — the **עֲרָלוֹת** (aralot, "foreskins") piled at the site of a national circumcision gave the hill its designation. The frank physicality of Hebrew place-naming is characteristic: the Hebrew Bible does not euphemize sacred geography. The act that happens at a place names the place.

---

### On 5:6 — אֶרֶץ זָבַת חָלָב וּדְבָשׁ: A Land Flowing Milk and Honey

**אֶרֶץ זָבַת חָלָב וּדְבָשׁ** — "a land flowing milk and honey."

The participial form **זָבַת** (zavat) from **זוּב** (zuv, "to flow, gush, ooze") modifies **אֶרֶץ** — the land itself flows. The image is of overabundance: the land is so rich that milk and honey flow from it as if from a body. This formula occurs more than twenty times in the Pentateuch (first in Exod. 3:8, 17; 13:5; 33:3; Lev. 20:24; Num. 13:27; 14:8; 16:13-14; Deut. 6:3; 11:9; 26:9, 15; 27:3; 31:20) and here in Joshua for the first time as the people stand on the land itself. What was promised in Exodus is now underfoot. The **חָלָב** (chalav, "milk") likely refers to goat's milk (sign of pastoral abundance) and **דְבָשׁ** (devash, "honey") may refer to date-honey or bee-honey (sign of natural sweetness) — together they denote agricultural and pastoral fecundity.

---

### On 5:9 — גַּלּוֹתִי / גִּלְגָּל: I Have Rolled / Gilgal (Key Crux — Wordplay)

**גַּלּוֹתִי אֶת-חֶרְפַּת מִצְרַיִם מֵעֲלֵיכֶם** — "I have rolled away the reproach of Egypt from upon you."

The verb **גַּלּוֹתִי** (galloti) is from the root **גָּלַל** (galal, "to roll, roll away"). The name **גִּלְגָּל** (Gilgal) is derived from the same root — it is a **gilgal**, a "rolling" or "circle" (perhaps originally a circle of stones). The wordplay is explicit and forms an etiology: the place is called Gilgal because YHWH "rolled away" (galloti) the reproach of Egypt there.

**חֶרְפַּת מִצְרַיִם** — "the reproach of Egypt." The meaning of this phrase is debated:

1. **The reproach that Egypt cast upon Israel** — the shame of servitude, the Egyptian mockery of Israel as a slave-people who could never possess a land or practice their covenant. Circumcision removes this reproach because it marks Israel as the covenant people of YHWH, no longer defined by Egyptian bondage.

2. **The reproach of being like the Egyptians** — the uncircumcised state. Since the wilderness generation had not been circumcised, they bore the mark (or rather the absence of a mark) of the uncircumcised — like the Egyptians (though this is complicated by the fact that Egyptians did practice circumcision in some periods). The circumcision at Gilgal removes this ambiguity.

3. **The reproach that Egypt would speak** — the potential mockery that YHWH brought his people out to die in the wilderness (cf. Exod. 32:12; Num. 14:13-16; Deut. 9:28). By bringing Israel into the land and renewing the covenant sign, YHWH has refuted this potential taunt.

The EMET preserves the phrase without resolving its ambiguity. The text itself does not specify which sense is primary — the rolling away of reproach encompasses all of these.

---

### On 5:10-12 — The Passover and the Cessation of Manna

**5:10-12** records a pivotal liturgical and providential transition: the first Passover in the promised land, followed by the cessation of the manna.

**מִמָּחֳרַת הַפֶּסַח** — "on the morrow of the Passover." This is the fifteenth of the first month (Nisan/Abib), the day after the Passover sacrifice on the fourteenth. On this day they ate **מֵעֲבוּר הָאָרֶץ** — "from the produce of the land." The word **עֲבוּר** (avur) means "produce, grain, yield" — the stored grain of the land of Canaan, presumably from the previous year's harvest left by the inhabitants.

**מַצּוֹת וְקָלוּי** — "unleavened cakes and parched grain." These are the foods of the Feast of Unleavened Bread (Matzot). **קָלוּי** (qalui, "parched/roasted grain") is grain dried and roasted — a simple preparation requiring no leavening. The eating of the land's produce on the day after Passover fulfills the agricultural promise: the people eat what the land yields rather than what heaven drops.

**וַיִּשְׁבֹּת הַמָּן** — "and the manna ceased." The verb **שָׁבַת** (shavat, "to cease, rest, stop") — the same root as Sabbath — is used for the manna's cessation. The manna, which began in Exodus 16:14-15 (approximately forty years earlier), now **rests** — it ceases its work. The manna was wilderness food, the bread of heaven for the journey; now that the journey is complete and the land is entered, the provisional gives way to the permanent. The timing is precise: the manna ceases **on the morrow** (the sixteenth of Nisan), the day after they first eat the land's produce. There is no gap — heaven's provision stops exactly when the land's provision begins. The transition is seamless: YHWH who fed them with manna in the wilderness now feeds them through the land's harvest.

---

### On 5:13-15 — שַׂר-צְבָא-יְהוָה: The Commander of the Army of YHWH (Key Crux)

**וְהִנֵּה-אִישׁ עֹמֵד לְנֶגְדּוֹ וְחַרְבּוֹ שְׁלוּפָה בְּיָדוֹ** — "and look — a man standing before him and his sword drawn in his hand."

This theophanic encounter is one of the most enigmatic passages in Joshua. Joshua sees **אִישׁ** (ish, "a man") — not an angel, not a divine figure, simply "a man" — standing opposite him with a drawn sword. The drawn sword indicates a warrior ready for combat. Joshua's question is the question of a field commander: "Are you for us or for our adversaries?" — **הֲלָנוּ אַתָּה אִם-לְצָרֵינוּ** — the binary of warfare, the demand to know on which side this armed figure stands.

**לֹא** — "No." The answer is the most theologically charged single word in the passage. The commander does not say "I am for you" — he says **לֹא** — "No." He refuses the binary. He is not "for" Israel in the sense that Joshua means — he is not a recruit to Israel's army, not an ally to be counted in their muster. He is **שַׂר-צְבָא-יְהוָה** — "commander of the army of YHWH." The army is YHWH's, not Israel's; the commander serves YHWH's purposes, not Israel's military strategy. The "No" reframes the entire conquest: Israel does not have a divine ally helping them fight their war — YHWH is conducting his own campaign, and Israel participates in it.

**שַׂר-צְבָא-יְהוָה** — "commander of the army of YHWH." The word **שַׂר** (sar) means "prince, commander, chief officer." **צְבָא** (tseva, "army, host") — the same word as in the divine title **יְהוָה צְבָאוֹת** (YHWH of Hosts/Armies). The commander of YHWH's army is the heavenly counterpart to Joshua's role as commander of Israel's army. The identity of this figure is debated:

1. **An angel of high rank** — a created being who commands the angelic hosts (cf. Dan. 10:13, 21; 12:1 where Michael is called **הַשַּׂר הַגָּדוֹל** — "the great prince").

2. **The Angel of YHWH** (מַלְאַךְ יְהוָה) — the figure who appeared to Moses at the burning bush, to whom divine attributes are ascribed. The parallel with Exodus 3 is unmistakable: in both passages, the figure commands the removal of sandals because the ground is holy (5:15 // Exod. 3:5).

3. **A pre-incarnate divine manifestation** — YHWH himself appearing in warrior form. Joshua's prostration (**וַיִּשְׁתָּחוּ**, "and he bowed down/worshipped") is not rebuked — unlike the angel's rebuke in Revelation 19:10; 22:8-9. The absence of rebuke has been read as evidence that this figure accepts worship because he is divine.

The EMET does not resolve the identification but notes the Exodus 3 parallel as the most significant textual key.

**שַׁל-נַעַלְךָ מֵעַל רַגְלֶךָ כִּי הַמָּקוֹם אֲשֶׁר אַתָּה עֹמֵד עָלָיו קֹדֶשׁ הוּא** — "Remove your sandal from upon your foot — for the place upon which you are standing — holy it is."

The command is nearly identical to Exodus 3:5: **שַׁל-נְעָלֶיךָ מֵעַל רַגְלֶיךָ כִּי הַמָּקוֹם אֲשֶׁר אַתָּה עוֹמֵד עָלָיו אַדְמַת-קֹדֶשׁ הוּא** — "Remove your sandals from upon your feet, for the place upon which you are standing — ground of holiness it is." The verbal echo is deliberate: Joshua is being placed in Moses's role. As Moses received his commission before the conquest of Egypt at the burning bush, so Joshua receives his commission before the conquest of Jericho at this theophany. The removal of sandals marks the ground as sacred — not inherently, but because of the divine presence standing upon it. Holiness is contagious: wherever YHWH stands, the ground becomes holy.

Note the singular **נַעַלְךָ** ("your sandal") and **רַגְלֶךָ** ("your foot") in Joshua 5:15, versus the plural **נְעָלֶיךָ** ("your sandals") and **רַגְלֶיךָ** ("your feet") in Exodus 3:5. The Masoretic text preserves this distinction; some versions harmonize to the plural.

The chapter ends abruptly — **וַיַּעַשׂ יְהוֹשֻׁעַ כֵּן** ("and Joshua did so") — without recording what the commander said next. The Septuagint (LXX) at this point includes additional material from Joshua 6:2-5 (YHWH's instructions for the fall of Jericho), suggesting that the chapter division here is artificial and that the theophany originally continued directly into the Jericho instructions. The MT's abrupt ending leaves the reader suspended in the encounter — an effective literary silence.

---

### On 5:4-7 — The Wilderness Generation and the Logic of Circumcision

The parenthetical explanation in vv. 4-7 is one of the most complex passages in Joshua syntactically. The Hebrew shifts between those who "went out" (הַיֹּצֵא/הַיֹּצְאִים) from Egypt and those who were "born" (הַיִּלֹּדִים) in the wilderness, creating a careful distinction between the two generations:

- **Generation 1** (the exodus generation): circumcised in Egypt, died in the wilderness due to disobedience (v. 6: "they did not listen to the voice of YHWH"). All the males of military age who left Egypt perished during the forty years of wandering.

- **Generation 2** (the wilderness-born): born during the journey, never circumcised. The text does not explain why they were not circumcised during the wilderness period — it simply states the fact: **לֹא-מָלוּ** ("they did not circumcise"). Rabbinic tradition offers several explanations: the danger of the journey made circumcision impractical (the north wind, which aids healing, did not blow during the wilderness years — Talmud Yevamot 71b-72a), or the covenant sign was suspended during the period of divine displeasure.

The circumcision at Gilgal thus reconstitutes Israel as a covenant community: the generation that enters the land bears the covenant sign in their flesh, as Abraham's household did when first commanded (Gen. 17:23-27).

---

## LXX Comparison Summary for Joshua 5

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Codex Vaticanus) | Notes |
|-------|---------------------|----------------------|-------|
| 5:1 | מַלְכֵי הָאֱמֹרִי ... מַלְכֵי הַכְּנַעֲנִי | βασιλεῖς τῶν Αμορραίων ... βασιλεῖς τῆς Φοινίκης | LXX: "kings of Phoenicia" for "kings of the Canaanites" — the LXX identifies the Canaanites with the Phoenicians, reflecting later geographical usage |
| 5:1 | וַיִּמַּס לְבָבָם | καὶ κατεπλάγησαν | LXX: "and they were struck with amazement/terror" — κατεπλάγησαν is weaker than "melted"; the visceral image of the heart dissolving is lost |
| 5:2 | חַרְבוֹת צֻרִים | μαχαίρας πετρίνας | LXX: "stone knives" — πέτρινος ("stone") for צֻרִים ("flints"). Accurate but generic |
| 5:2 | שֵׁנִית | ἐκ δευτέρου | LXX: "a second time" — exact rendering |
| 5:9 | גַּלּוֹתִי / גִּלְגָּל | (wordplay lost) | The galal/Gilgal etymological wordplay cannot be reproduced in Greek. The LXX renders the place-name as Γαλγαλα (transliteration) |
| 5:11 | מֵעֲבוּר הָאָרֶץ | τοῦ σίτου τῆς γῆς | LXX: "the grain of the land" — σῖτος (wheat/grain) for עֲבוּר (produce). LXX specifies grain where Hebrew is more general |
| 5:14 | לֹא כִּי אֲנִי שַׂר-צְבָא-יְהוָה | ἐγὼ ἀρχιστράτηγος δυνάμεως κυρίου | LXX: "I [am] the commander-in-chief of the power/army of the Lord" — ἀρχιστράτηγος is a stronger military title than Hebrew שַׂר; κυρίου for YHWH |
| 5:15 | שַׁל-נַעַלְךָ | λῦσαι τὸ ὑπόδημα | LXX: "loose/untie the sandal" — λύω (to loose) vs. MT שַׁל (to remove/slip off). The LXX implies the sandal is tied on and must be untied; the Hebrew implies it is simply removed |
| 5:15 | נַעַלְךָ ... רַגְלֶךָ (singular) | τὸ ὑπόδημα ... τῶν ποδῶν σου (plural) | LXX harmonizes to the plural "sandals ... feet," matching the Exodus 3:5 phrasing. MT preserves the singular, which may be the harder (and therefore more original) reading |

---

## Dead Sea Scrolls and Versional Notes

**Dead Sea Scrolls:** A fragmentary copy of Joshua (4QJosh-a = 4Q47) preserves portions of Joshua 5, though the text is heavily damaged. The extant fragments show general agreement with the MT, with minor orthographic variations. The most notable Qumran evidence for Joshua is the "Apocryphon of Joshua" (4Q378-379), which paraphrases rather than copies the biblical text.

**Targum Jonathan on 5:2:** Renders **חַרְבוֹת צֻרִים** as "sharp knives" (סכינין חריפין) — interpreting **צֻרִים** as "sharp" rather than "flint," avoiding the archaic material specification. The Targum's interpretive move suggests that by the Aramaic period, the flint specification was felt to require explanation.

**Targum Jonathan on 5:14:** Renders **שַׂר-צְבָא-יְהוָה** as "the angel/messenger who is commander of the army of YHWH" — explicitly identifying the figure as an angel, removing the ambiguity of the Hebrew **אִישׁ** ("man"). This interpretive identification is standard in rabbinic tradition.

**Josephus (Antiquities V.1.4):** Josephus records the circumcision at Gilgal, the Passover, and the cessation of the manna. He does not record the theophany of 5:13-15, either omitting it deliberately or following a text that placed it differently.

**Rashi on 5:2:** Notes that **שֵׁנִית** ("a second time") does not mean each person was circumcised twice but that the circumcision was performed "again" — meaning the nation was circumcised again as a collective act, the first time being in Egypt before the Exodus.

**On the singular sandal (5:15):** The Masoretic singular **נַעַלְךָ** ("your sandal") against the Exodus 3:5 plural **נְעָלֶיךָ** ("your sandals") has generated discussion. Some suggest Joshua removed only one sandal (the right foot, in a gesture of deference or land-claim — cf. Ruth 4:7-8 where sandal-removal signifies transfer of right). Others regard it as a simple variant. The EMET preserves the singular as given in the Masoretic text.
